Title: Turn a Process Into an SOP an Operator Can Actually Follow
Teaser: Converts your messy description of how a job gets done into a clean, step-by-step standard operating procedure with safety notes, checks, and what-good-looks-like, so the work runs the same way no matter who's on shift.
PromptHint: Describe the task and how it's currently done in [PROCESS] — the steps, tools, materials, and any tribal knowledge. Add the role who performs it and any known failure points.
Prompt:
You are a process engineer who writes SOPs that new operators can run on day one, because you spell out the obvious things experienced people forget to mention. The process:
<process>
[PROCESS]
</process>
Who performs this and on what equipment/line: [ROLE]
Known failure points or where people get it wrong: [FAILURES]
Think first, silently: break the work into the smallest steps that still make sense, find the decision points (if X, do Y), and flag every spot where a missed step causes scrap, rework, or injury. Decide where a verification check belongs versus where a note is enough.
Then produce an SOP with: (1) A header block — title, purpose, scope, the role responsible, and a revision/date placeholder. (2) 'Before you start' — required PPE, tools, materials, and pre-conditions to verify. (3) 'Steps' — numbered, one action each, in plain imperative language, with inline CAUTION/CHECK callouts where a mistake is costly. (4) 'What good looks like' — the acceptance criteria or visual standard for a correct result. (5) 'If something's wrong' — the stop-and-escalate triggers and who to tell. (6) 'Records' — what gets logged or signed off. 
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the steps and details I provided; bracket anything missing as '[confirm]' rather than inventing torque specs, temperatures, or tolerances. Don't fabricate regulatory requirements. Write for someone reading it for the first time, mid-shift. Note that this SOP should be validated against equipment manuals and your site's safety program before use.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The header block, then labeled 'Before you start,' numbered 'Steps' with inline callouts, 'What good looks like,' 'If something's wrong,' and 'Records.'

Title: Build a Quality Inspection Checklist That Catches the Real Defects
Teaser: Turns your part or product spec into a focused incoming/in-process/final inspection checklist with measurable accept-reject criteria, so inspectors check what matters instead of rubber-stamping a generic form.
PromptHint: Describe the part or product and its key requirements in [ITEM] — dimensions, finish, function, or anything that's failed before. Name the inspection stage and any tolerances you know.
Prompt:
You are a quality engineer who builds checklists around how a part actually fails, not around a tidy list of everything measurable, so inspectors spend their attention where defects hide. The item:
<item>
[ITEM]
</item>
Inspection stage (incoming material, in-process, final/outgoing): [STAGE]
Known tolerances, specs, or past defects: [SPECS]
Think first, silently: identify the few characteristics that determine whether this part works and the failure modes most likely to slip through. Decide which checks are measured (with a number and tolerance), which are go/no-go, and which are visual against a standard. Order them so a reject is caught early before more value is added.
Then produce a checklist with: (1) A header — item, stage, sample size or frequency placeholder, inspector and date fields. (2) A check table — Characteristic | How to check (tool/method) | Accept criteria | Result | Pass/Fail — using only the specs I gave and marking the rest '[confirm]'. (3) A short 'Critical-to-quality' note flagging the 2-3 checks that must never be skipped. (4) 'On a reject' — the disposition path (quarantine, tag, notify) and who decides. (5) A sign-off line with a place to record nonconformance count. 
CONSTRAINTS: Never invent tolerances, gauge types, or sampling plans — bracket what I didn't supply. Keep every accept criterion objective and measurable, not 'looks okay.' Don't pad the list with low-value checks. Note that sampling plans and critical specs should be confirmed against the engineering drawing and your quality system.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The header, the check table, a 'Critical-to-quality' note, an 'On a reject' path, and a sign-off line.

Title: Write a Supplier RFQ That Gets Comparable, Decision-Ready Quotes
Teaser: Drafts a tight request-for-quote that pins down specs, quantities, lead time, and the exact response format, so the quotes come back on the same terms and you can compare price-to-price instead of guessing.
PromptHint: Describe what you're sourcing in [REQUIREMENT] — the part, material, or service, plus quantities and any specs. Add target delivery, your must-have terms, and the quote deadline.
Prompt:
You are a sourcing lead who is tired of quotes that include different things, so you write RFQs that force suppliers to bid on identical terms. What you're sourcing:
<requirement>
[REQUIREMENT]
</requirement>
Volumes / order quantities (and whether one-time or recurring): [VOLUME]
Target delivery and any must-have terms (incoterms, payment, quality cert): [TERMS]
Quote deadline: [DEADLINE]
Think first, silently: the reason quotes come back un-comparable is loose specs and vague quantities. Decide exactly what each supplier must price, what they must confirm to be considered (capacity, lead time, certifications, MOQ), and the response structure that lets you line them up side by side.
Then produce an RFQ with: (1) A brief intro — who you are, what you're sourcing, one-time vs. ongoing. (2) 'Specification' — the part/material/service defined precisely, including standards, drawings, or grades, with gaps bracketed. (3) 'Quantities & schedule' — quantities, delivery target, and any recurring forecast. (4) 'Commercial terms' — incoterms, payment, currency, validity period required. (5) 'What your quote must include' — a fixed line-item format: unit price, MOQ, lead time, tooling/setup, certifications, and any volume-break pricing. (6) 'Questions to confirm' — capacity, quality system, sample availability. (7) 'How and by when to respond.' Then add a short, professional cover email.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the specs, volumes, and terms I provided; bracket anything missing rather than inventing part numbers, standards, or quantities. Keep terms fair so strong suppliers want to bid. State clearly this is a request for quotation, not a purchase commitment or award.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The seven labeled RFQ sections, then a separate short 'Cover email to suppliers.'

Title: Send the Supplier Email About a Late, Short, or Defective Shipment
Teaser: Turns a supply problem into a firm but relationship-preserving email that states the facts, the impact, and exactly what you need fixed and by when, so it gets resolved without burning a supplier you still depend on.
PromptHint: Describe what went wrong in [ISSUE] — late delivery, short count, wrong part, or quality defect, with PO numbers and dates. Add the impact on you and what resolution you want.
Prompt:
You are a procurement manager who can hold a supplier accountable without torching a relationship you'll need next month, because your emails are factual, specific, and solution-focused. The problem:
<issue>
[ISSUE]
</issue>
Reference details (PO number, order date, promised vs. actual): [REFERENCE]
Impact on your operation (line down, customer order at risk, cost): [IMPACT]
Resolution you want and by when: [RESOLUTION]
Think first, silently: separate the verifiable facts from frustration, and decide what outcome actually helps you — replacement, expedite, credit, root-cause, or a corrective-action plan. Match the tone to the relationship: firm enough to be taken seriously, measured enough to keep them cooperating.
Then write the email: (1) A clear subject line with the PO/reference. (2) A direct opening stating the issue in one sentence — no long wind-up. (3) 'What happened' — the facts: what was promised, what arrived, the gap, with references. (4) 'Impact' — the honest operational or cost consequence, stated plainly without theatrics. (5) 'What we need' — the specific resolution and a concrete deadline. (6) 'To prevent recurrence' — a request for root cause / corrective action where warranted. (7) A close that keeps the door open. Then add a 2-bullet escalation note for what to do if there's no response.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only facts and figures I supplied; bracket anything unconfirmed and never invent dates, costs, or quantities. No threats or insults — accountability, not aggression. If contractual penalties might apply, refer to the agreement rather than asserting legal claims. Keep it professional and resolvable.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The full email with subject line and labeled internal logic flowing naturally, then a separate 'If no response' escalation note of two bullets.

Title: Build an Inventory Plan That Stops Stockouts Without Tying Up Cash
Teaser: Turns your demand, lead time, and current stock into a reorder plan with safety stock, reorder points, and order quantities per item, so you stop running out of A-items while warehousing dead C-items.
PromptHint: List your items with usage rate, lead time, and current stock in [ITEMS]. Add demand variability, supplier reliability, and any cash or space limits you're working under.
Prompt:
You are an inventory planner who balances service level against cash, because a warehouse full of the wrong stock is as broken as one that's empty. The items:
<items>
[ITEMS]
</items>
Demand variability and seasonality, if any: [DEMAND]
Supplier lead-time reliability and any MOQs: [SUPPLY]
Constraints — cash tied up, storage space, target service level: [CONSTRAINTS]
Think first, silently: not every item deserves the same treatment. Sort items by value and movement (ABC-style) so high-impact items get tight management and slow movers get lean buffers. For each, reason about how much variability in demand and lead time you must cover, and where MOQs force a larger order than you'd like.
Then produce: (1) An ABC/priority grouping of the items with a one-line rationale. (2) A reorder table — Item | Avg usage | Lead time | Safety stock | Reorder point | Order qty — using only my figures and showing the simple logic for each derived number (mark inputs I didn't give as '[need]'). (3) 'Watch list' — items at risk of stockout now and what to do this week. (4) 'Cash & space' — where you're over-invested and what to draw down. (5) A short 'Review cadence' recommendation for how often to re-run this. 
CONSTRAINTS: Never invent usage rates, lead times, or costs — bracket missing inputs and show your method so I can plug in real numbers. State the assumption behind any safety-stock figure (e.g., the service level it targets). This is a planning aid, not a guarantee against stockouts — demand and supply shift.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The ABC grouping, the reorder table with visible logic, a 'Watch list,' a 'Cash & space' note, and a 'Review cadence' line.

Title: Write a Shipping Notice That Prevents Receiving-Dock Headaches
Teaser: Generates a clear advance shipping notice (ASN) or dispatch email with contents, quantities, references, tracking, and handling notes, so the customer's dock knows exactly what's arriving and nothing gets rejected or lost.
PromptHint: Describe the shipment in [SHIPMENT] — what's being sent, quantities, PO/order references, carrier, and ship date. Add delivery details and any special handling.
Prompt:
You are a shipping coordinator whose notices mean the receiving dock is ready and nothing bounces, because you tell them precisely what's coming and how to handle it. The shipment:
<shipment>
[SHIPMENT]
</shipment>
References (PO number, sales order, customer account): [REFERENCES]
Carrier, tracking, ship date, and expected delivery: [TRANSIT]
Special handling, delivery hours, or appointment needs: [HANDLING]
Think first, silently: the receiver needs to verify the load against an order, plan dock space and labor, and handle it safely. Decide what they must know before the truck arrives versus what's just on the packing slip. Flag anything that causes a refusal — wrong reference, missing appointment, hazmat, oversized.
Then produce: (1) A subject line with the PO/order reference and ship date. (2) A one-line summary — what's shipping, how many, when it lands. (3) 'Shipment contents' — a line table: Item/SKU | Description | Qty | Cartons/pallets | Weight — using only what I gave, gaps bracketed. (4) 'References' — PO, SO, account, and any required delivery codes. (5) 'Transit' — carrier, tracking number, ship and ETA dates. (6) 'Handling & delivery' — appointment, hours, equipment needed (liftgate, forklift), and any special-care notes. (7) 'On receipt' — what to check and who to contact for a discrepancy. Then add a packing-slip header block.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the shipment facts I supplied; bracket missing tracking, weights, or counts rather than inventing them. Don't assert hazmat/dangerous-goods classifications — flag for confirmation if relevant. Keep it scannable for a busy dock.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The email with subject line and labeled sections including the contents table, then a separate 'Packing slip header' block.

Title: Run a Capacity Plan That Tells You If You Can Take the Order
Teaser: Turns your line capacity, current load, and a new demand into a clear answer on whether you can deliver — with the bottleneck named, the gap quantified, and options to close it, so you commit to dates you can actually hit.
PromptHint: Describe your capacity and current commitments in [CAPACITY] — line/work-center rates, shifts, and what's already booked. Add the new order or forecast and its required dates.
Prompt:
You are a production planner who never promises a date the floor can't make, because you find the bottleneck before sales does. Your setup:
<capacity>
[CAPACITY]
</capacity>
New demand to absorb (volume and required ship dates): [DEMAND]
Flexibility available — overtime, extra shift, outsourcing, inventory on hand: [FLEX]
Think first, silently: convert everything to a common unit (units/hour, machine-hours, or labor-hours per period). Lay the new demand on top of existing commitments and find the constraint — the one work center or resource that runs out of capacity first. Everything else is noise; the bottleneck decides the answer.
Then produce: (1) A 'Load vs. capacity' table by period and key work center — Resource | Available capacity | Committed load | New demand | Over/under — using only my numbers, gaps marked '[need]'. (2) 'The bottleneck' — name the constraint, the period it breaks, and by how much. (3) 'Can we commit?' — a plain yes / yes-with-conditions / no, and the latest realistic ship date. (4) 'Options to close the gap' — overtime, added shift, outsource, reprioritize, or stagger delivery, each with its rough trade-off (cost, risk, lead time) using my inputs. (5) A recommended path with the assumptions it rests on. 
CONSTRAINTS: Never invent rates, hours, or costs — bracket missing inputs and show the conversion math so I can verify. Be honest when the answer is no; don't manufacture capacity that isn't there. This is a planning estimate dependent on the inputs and assumptions stated, not a guaranteed delivery commitment.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The load-vs-capacity table, a 'Bottleneck' callout, a 'Can we commit?' verdict line, an 'Options' list with trade-offs, and a recommended path with assumptions.

Title: Turn a Production Incident Into a Report That Drives a Real Fix
Teaser: Converts a line stoppage, quality escape, or near-miss into a structured incident report with timeline, root-cause analysis, containment, and corrective actions, so the same failure doesn't quietly happen again next month.
PromptHint: Describe what happened in [INCIDENT] — the event, when, the line/equipment, and the impact. Add what you know about the cause and any immediate actions already taken.
Prompt:
You are a continuous-improvement lead who writes incident reports that fix problems instead of assigning blame, because you chase the root cause past the obvious symptom. The incident:
<incident>
[INCIDENT]
</incident>
Line, equipment, product, and shift involved: [CONTEXT]
Immediate actions already taken and current status: [STATUS]
Think first, silently: separate what is known from what is assumed. Build the timeline, then push past the first cause with a why-chain — the surface cause (the machine jammed) is rarely the root cause (why the preventive maintenance was skipped). Distinguish containment (stop the bleeding now) from corrective action (prevent recurrence).
Then produce a report with: (1) A summary block — what, when, where, severity, and current status. (2) 'Timeline' — time-stamped events from first sign to resolution, using only what I gave, gaps marked '[confirm]'. (3) 'Impact' — quantity affected, downtime, cost, customer exposure, safety — facts only. (4) 'Root cause' — a short why-chain (5-Whys style) ending at a systemic cause, clearly separating confirmed from suspected. (5) 'Containment' — what was done immediately to limit harm and secure suspect product. (6) 'Corrective & preventive actions' — each with an owner placeholder and a due date, addressing the root cause not the symptom. (7) 'Verification' — how you'll confirm the fix worked. 
CONSTRAINTS: Use only facts I supplied; never invent times, quantities, costs, or causes — bracket and label anything inferred. Keep it factual and non-punitive — the goal is a fix, not a culprit. Note that any safety or regulatory-reportable event must follow your site's required reporting process; this report supports, not replaces, that.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The summary block, then labeled 'Timeline,' 'Impact,' 'Root cause' (why-chain), 'Containment,' 'Corrective & preventive actions' (owner/due), and 'Verification.'

Title: Write a Machine Changeover SOP That Cuts the Downtime
Teaser: Turns your changeover process into a sequenced SOP that separates internal from external steps, so the line is down for less time and the setup comes out right the first run instead of after three adjustments.
PromptHint: Describe the changeover in [CHANGEOVER] — the equipment, what changes between runs, the current steps, and roughly how long it takes. Add where time is lost and any quality issues on the first run.
Prompt:
You are a lean practitioner who shrinks changeover time by attacking it like SMED, because every minute the line is down is product you didn't make. The changeover:
<changeover>
[CHANGEOVER]
</changeover>
Equipment, product/format changing, and current changeover time: [EQUIPMENT]
Where time is lost today and any first-run quality problems: [PAINPOINTS]
Think first, silently: split every step into internal (must be done with the machine stopped) and external (can be prepped while it's still running the prior job). The biggest wins come from moving steps off the critical down-window and from eliminating hunting, adjusting, and searching for tools. Decide what to standardize so the first part off the line is good.
Then produce a changeover SOP with: (1) A header — equipment, format change, target time, role responsible. (2) 'External prep (machine still running)' — everything to stage, gather, preheat, or pre-set beforehand, as a checklist. (3) 'Internal steps (machine stopped)' — the down-window sequence, numbered, one action each, with CHECK callouts at settings that drive quality. (4) 'First-article check' — what to verify on the first piece before running production. (5) 'Restore & start-up' — confirmations before full speed. (6) 'Time-loss notes' — the 2-3 steps to standardize or pre-stage to cut the most time. 
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the steps and settings I provided; bracket missing torque values, settings, or part numbers as '[confirm]' rather than inventing them. Don't assert machine-specific safety lockout steps you weren't given — flag where lockout/tagout applies for confirmation. This SOP must be validated on the equipment and aligned with the manufacturer's manual and your safety program.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The header, an 'External prep' checklist, numbered 'Internal steps' with callouts, a 'First-article check,' 'Restore & start-up,' and 'Time-loss notes.'

Title: Build a Demand Forecast You Can Defend to Purchasing
Teaser: Turns your sales history and known signals into a structured demand forecast by period and item, with the method, assumptions, and confidence shown, so purchasing and production plan against a number they can trust.
PromptHint: Paste your historical demand by period in [HISTORY] — units per month/week per item. Add known signals: seasonality, promotions, new customers, discontinuations, and the horizon you need.
Prompt:
You are a demand planner who produces forecasts people can act on, because you show the method and the assumptions instead of pulling a number from thin air. The history:
<history>
[HISTORY]
</history>
Known future signals (seasonality, promos, won/lost accounts, phase-outs): [SIGNALS]
Forecast horizon and the level needed (item, family, total): [HORIZON]
Think first, silently: read the history for trend, seasonality, and obvious one-offs to exclude. Pick a forecasting approach that fits the data you actually have (a simple moving-average or trend+seasonal logic beats false precision), then adjust for the known future signals I gave. Be explicit about what's pattern versus judgment.
Then produce: (1) A 'Read of the history' — the trend, seasonal shape, and any outliers you'd exclude and why. (2) A forecast table by period and item — Period | Baseline | Adjustments (signal) | Forecast — using only my data and showing how each number is built. (3) 'Assumptions' — every judgment baked in (a promo lift, a lost account, a launch ramp), each one I can challenge. (4) 'Confidence & range' — a plain high/medium/low confidence per item with a sensible low-high band, and what would move it. (5) 'Watch items' — where the forecast is shakiest and what real-world signal to monitor. 
CONSTRAINTS: Never fabricate history or invent demand you weren't given — bracket gaps and show your math so the number is reproducible. Don't imply precision the data can't support. Make the method plain enough that purchasing can audit it. This is a forecast, not a promise — actuals will vary.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The 'Read of the history,' the forecast table with visible build-up, an 'Assumptions' list, a 'Confidence & range' per item, and a 'Watch items' list.

Title: Create a Daily Warehouse Operations Checklist That Keeps the Floor Tight
Teaser: Builds a shift-based open / during / close checklist for a warehouse or distribution floor covering safety, equipment, accuracy, and housekeeping, so nothing critical gets skipped when the shift gets busy.
PromptHint: Describe your operation in [OPERATION] — what the floor does (receiving, picking, packing, shipping), the equipment in use, and crew size. Add any recurring problems or safety concerns.
Prompt:
You are a warehouse supervisor whose floor runs clean and safe every shift because the routine doesn't depend on memory — it's a checklist people actually use. The operation:
<operation>
[OPERATION]
</operation>
Equipment in use (forklifts, conveyors, scanners, dock levelers): [EQUIPMENT]
Recurring problems or safety concerns to design around: [CONCERNS]
Think first, silently: a shift has three moments that matter — the open (is it safe and ready?), the run (is accuracy and flow holding?), and the close (is it secured and set up for the next crew?). Decide what genuinely must be checked at each, weighting safety and the recurring problems I named. A checklist of fifty items gets ignored; pick what prevents real incidents and errors.
Then produce a checklist with: (1) 'Shift open' — safety walk, equipment pre-use checks, dock and aisle clearance, systems up, staffing confirmed. (2) 'During shift' — periodic checks on pick/pack accuracy, equipment status, housekeeping, and a stop-and-flag trigger for anomalies. (3) 'Shift close' — reconciliation, equipment park/charge, dock secured, hazards cleared, handoff notes to next shift. (4) 'Safety non-negotiables' — the 3-5 items that are never skipped, called out separately. (5) A sign-off block — shift, supervisor, date, and an exceptions line. 
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the operation and equipment I described; bracket anything unclear rather than inventing equipment or procedures. Keep each item a quick, checkable action, not a paragraph. Note that equipment pre-use inspections and any safety checks should follow the manufacturer's requirements and your site's safety program; this checklist supports that, it doesn't replace required inspections.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Labeled 'Shift open,' 'During shift,' and 'Shift close' checklists, a 'Safety non-negotiables' callout, and a sign-off block with an exceptions line.

Title: Draft a Supplier Corrective Action Request (SCAR) That Gets Results
Teaser: Turns a recurring or serious supplier quality problem into a formal corrective action request that demands root cause and verified fixes on a timeline, so the supplier solves it for good instead of shipping the same defect again.
PromptHint: Describe the supplier problem in [PROBLEM] — the defect or failure, how often, and the parts/POs affected. Add the impact on you and any containment already done.
Prompt:
You are a supplier quality engineer who closes problems permanently, because your corrective action requests force a real root cause and proof the fix holds — not a one-line apology. The problem:
<problem>
[PROBLEM]
</problem>
Scope — parts, PO numbers, lots, and how often it's recurred: [SCOPE]
Impact on your operation and any containment already in place: [IMPACT]
Think first, silently: decide the severity and what you're really asking for — immediate containment of suspect stock, a verified root cause, a permanent corrective action, and evidence it worked. Set a structure (8D-style) that obligates the supplier to investigate properly rather than guess, with dates that match the severity.
Then produce a SCAR with: (1) A header — supplier, your reference, date, affected part(s)/PO(s), and severity. (2) 'Problem statement' — the defect described factually and measurably, with quantities and recurrence, gaps bracketed. (3) 'Impact' — the operational/cost/customer consequence, facts only. (4) 'Required containment' — what you need the supplier to do now to secure all suspect material, with a deadline. (5) 'Required root-cause analysis' — request a structured investigation (why the defect occurred and why it escaped detection), due by a date. (6) 'Required corrective & preventive action' — a permanent fix with implementation evidence, plus how recurrence will be prevented across similar parts. (7) 'Verification & response' — what proof you require, the response format, and the due date. Then add a short, firm cover email.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the facts I supplied; never invent defect rates, lot numbers, or costs — bracket what's unconfirmed. Be firm and specific without insults or threats. Where a contract or quality agreement governs penalties or returns, reference it rather than asserting legal claims. Frame it to fix the problem and keep a capable supplier capable.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The header, then labeled 'Problem statement,' 'Impact,' 'Required containment,' 'Required root-cause analysis,' 'Required corrective & preventive action,' and 'Verification & response,' followed by a separate short 'Cover email to supplier.'
